explanation: The input natural language describes the conditions of 'VentilationOff' mode: (i) the machine does not ventilate, (ii) the in valve is closed, and (iii) the out valve is opened. Given the atomic propositions, to capture 'VentilationOff', we must ensure that 'ventilating' is false (machine does not ventilate), 'inValveClose' is true (in valve closed), and 'outValveOpen' is true (out valve opened). To ensure this applies whenever 'StandbyMode' (which is also referred to as 'VentilationOff') is active, we formulate the property so that globally, whenever 'StandbyMode' holds, the three conditions above also hold at the same time. A previous formulation that didn't use the connection of StandbyMode and these atomics, or used F or X instead of G, would not globally capture the requirement whenever 'VentilationOff' (aka 'StandbyMode') is active. This LTL property captures the requirement as a global implication.

The input natural language specifies the conditions for 'ventilation off' mode: the machine does NOT ventilate, the in valve is closed, and the out valve is opened. In LTL, we can capture this as a state property: whenever the system is NOT ventilating (i.e., 'ventilating' is false), the in valve must be closed and the out valve must be open. Because there are atomic propositions available for 'inValveClose' and 'outValveOpen', and 'ventilating' for ventilation, the condition can be expressed as: globally, whenever it is not ventilating, inValveClose and outValveOpen hold. Thus, the LTL: G(!ventilating -> (inValveClose & outValveOpen)). This property directly captures the natural language requirement, expressing that it must always be the case whenever ventilation is off, the in valve is closed and the out valve is open. It also avoids using any numerical comparisons, as required.

The input describes the 'Ventilation Off' state, where the machine does not ventilate, the inspiratory valve is closed, and the out valve is open. In the provided atomic propositions, the relevant atoms are: 'ventilating' (ventilation is on), 'inValveClose' (inspiratory valve closed), and 'outValveOpen' (out valve open). The requirement is that in the 'Ventilation Off' condition, it must always be the case that the machine is not ventilating, the in valve is closed, and the out valve is open. Since there's no explicit atomic proposition for 'VentilationOff', we match based on available state. The strongest translation, then, is that whenever ventilation is off (i.e., 'ventilating' is FALSE), then both 'inValveClose' and 'outValveOpen' must hold. This is captured as: globally, whenever NOT ventilating, inValveClose and outValveOpen are true.

The input describes the 'VentilationOff' or standby state, where the machine does not ventilate (ventilating is false), the input (inspiratory) valve is closed, and the output (expiratory) valve is open. Assuming 'StandbyMode' means 'VentilationOff', we must ensure that when 'StandbyMode' is true, the three required conditions hold: not ventilating, inValveClose, and outValveOpen. The LTL property expresses that globally, whenever the system is in StandbyMode, these conditions are true at the same time. This LTL formula directly encodes the described behavior and does not rely on any other (possibly incomplete) atomic propositions such as 'failSafeMode' (which conceptually overlaps but is not identical to VentilationOff/Standby).

The input specifies the 'VentilationOff' condition, defined by three properties: (1) the machine does not ventilate, (2) the in valve is closed, and (3) the out valve is opened. Given the available atomic propositions, 'StandbyMode' is described as equivalent to 'VentilationOff', and in its mode, ventilation is off. To reflect the explicit conditions about the valves, we use 'inValveClose' and 'outValveOpen'. To encode this in LTL, we interpret that whenever StandbyMode is true, then the machine should not be ventilating ('ventilating' is false), the inspiratory valve should be closed, and the out valve should be open. Globally, whenever StandbyMode holds, these properties must also hold. This is modeled using the LTL formula globally (G), with an implication conditioned on StandbyMode.

The natural language statement defines the 'VentilationOff' mode: the machine does not ventilate, the in (inspiratory) valve is closed, and the out (expiratory) valve is open. There's no direct atomic proposition for 'VentilationOff', but 'StandbyMode' is described as 'same as VentilationOff Mode', thus, it is the best available proposition to use. To capture the requirements: (1) 'the machine does not ventilate' corresponds to '!ventilating'; (2) 'the in valve is closed' corresponds to 'inValveClose'; (3) 'the out valve is opened' corresponds to 'outValveOpen'. To ensure that when we are in StandbyMode, these properties always hold, we use the LTL formula: globally, whenever StandbyMode holds, then these conditions also hold, i.e., G(StandbyMode -> (!ventilating & inValveClose & outValveOpen)). This ensures at all times, being in VentilationOff/StandbyMode enforces the required properties. Previous or alternative LTL properties may have failed to require all of these conjunctively whenever StandbyMode holds, or may have omitted the link with StandbyMode, being too general or only requiring conditions at entry or not at all times.

The natural language requirement states that in the 'ventilation off' state (also called StandbyMode), the machine must not ventilate, the inspiratory (in) valve must be closed, and the expiratory (out) valve must be opened. There is an explicit atomic proposition 'StandbyMode' (synonymous with VentilationOff). Assuming StandbyMode means VentilationOff, the specification is that whenever StandbyMode is true, three things must also be true: not ventilating, in valve is closed, and out valve is open. In LTL, this is expressed using the globally (G) operator stating that it is always the case that if StandbyMode, then !ventilating, inValveClose, and outValveOpen all hold simultaneously. Expressions that use comparison operators (such as == or != or >) are not needed, only boolean (propositional) logic is used over atomic propositions.

The input describes the 'ventilation off' condition: when ventilation is off, the machine does not ventilate (so, 'ventilating' is false), the inspiratory valve is closed ('inValveClose' is true), and the expiratory (out) valve is open ('outValveOpen' is true). This behavior describes a particular mode - either 'StandbyMode' or whenever 'ventilating' is false. Since the atomic proposition 'StandbyMode' is explicitly defined as 'ventilation off' in the proposition descriptions (and matches the described behavior), we relate these conditions directly to 'StandbyMode'. So, whenever 'StandbyMode' holds, then at the same time 'ventilating' is false, 'inValveClose' is true, and 'outValveOpen' is true. The proper way to capture this is with a globally (G) operator: globally, if 'StandbyMode' is active ('StandbyMode'), then these conditions are enforced. The property is:G ( StandbyMode -> ( !ventilating & inValveClose & outValveOpen ) ). This captures the unambiguous mapping between the natural language and atomic propositions. Other possible LTLs that e.g. tie to 'failSafeMode' or only reference 'ventilating' without the specific mode do not capture the intent that these conditions are enforced specifically during 'ventilation off' (i.e., 'StandbyMode'), per the meanings in the atomic propositions.

The natural language describes the 'VentilationOff' (or StandbyMode) condition, in which the machine must not ventilate, the inspiratory (in) valve is closed, and the expiratory (out) valve is open. From the given atomic propositions, 'ventilating' represents the machine ventilating, 'inValveClose' indicates the inspiratory valve is closed, and 'outValveOpen' indicates the expiratory valve is open. To capture the invariant nature of this state, we require that whenever the system is in VentilationOff (which can be equated to StandbyMode based on the atomic proposition descriptions), it must be the case that the machine is not ventilating, the in valve is closed, and the out valve is open. This can be represented as: globally, if we are in StandbyMode, then those three conditions must simultaneously hold, i.e., G (StandbyMode -> (!ventilating & inValveClose & outValveOpen)).

The natural language says that in Ventilation Off (Standby) mode, the machine does not ventilate, the in valve is closed, and the out valve is open. Therefore, whenever the system is NOT ventilating, we want to ensure that the in valve is closed and the out valve is open. In LTL, this is captured by globally stating that if 'ventilating' is false (i.e., '!ventilating'), then both 'inValveClose' and 'outValveOpen' must be true at that moment. This property precisely expresses the required relationship during Ventilation Off. Previous LTLs might incorrectly combine modes (like StandbyMode) or ignore that the valves must correspond to the ventilation status regardless of mode naming, but this precisely encodes the behavior described.
